questions.question: What is the primary advantage of educational resources over one-on-one wisdom-sharing?

options.text: Educational resources are always more effective at creating transformation
Educational resources scale infinitely (one-to-many), are available 24/7, and exist beyond your lifetime
Educational resources are easier and faster to create than having conversations
Educational resources completely replace the need for direct interaction

questions.explanation: Educational resources offer scalability (one-to-many reach), 24/7 availability, existence beyond your lifetime, and ability to be updated over time. However, they're complementary to one-on-one interaction, not superior or replacement—resources provide scalable foundations while direct interaction provides personalization and depth. One-on-one has advantages (personalized, adaptive, relational) that resources can't replicate.

questions.question: What makes content 'evergreen' according to the lesson?

options.text: Content that focuses on current trends and platforms to stay relevant
Content that addresses fundamental human experiences and universal principles, remaining valuable across time
Content that never needs updating or maintenance
Content that is at least 10 years old

questions.explanation: Evergreen content remains valuable across time and changing circumstances because it addresses fundamental human experiences (grief, resilience, meaning-making) and universal principles rather than trendy tactics or dated specifics. The strategy is to create evergreen core content (70%) with timely examples/applications (30%) that can be updated. Even evergreen content needs periodic review and maintenance—it's not 'set and forget.'

questions.question: What is the 'backward design' process for creating educational content?

options.text: Start with activities you want to include, then figure out what learners will get from them
Start with desired learning outcomes, determine evidence of learning, THEN design the content and activities
Start by looking at what others have created and copying their approach
Start with the easiest content to create and build from there

questions.explanation: Backward design (Wiggins & McTighe) starts with the END in mind: (1) Identify desired learning outcomes—what should learners be able to DO?, (2) Determine evidence of learning—how will you know they achieved it?, (3) Design the learning experience working backward from the desired end state, (4) Build in feedback loops. This ensures content serves clear learning goals rather than just being 'stuff you know' organized randomly.

questions.question: What is the evidence grade for instructional design principles used in educational content creation?

options.text: WEAK - only anecdotal support
MODERATE - some evidence but significant limitations
STRONG - well-established through research in learning sciences
EMERGING - too new to evaluate properly

questions.explanation: Instructional design principles have STRONG evidence from learning sciences research. Backward design, adult learning principles, and user-centered design are evidence-based approaches. However, evidence for wisdom-sharing content specifically is LOW-MODERATE, and evergreen content strategy in legacy work is less researched. The core instructional design principles are solid; application to wisdom-sharing specifically is more emerging.

questions.question: When does content creation become harmful and require reassessment?

options.text: Whenever you create any educational resources about your experience
When you focus on quality over quantity and create only 1-3 deep resources
When content creation becomes compulsive, self-worth is tied to performance metrics, or you neglect relationships/self-care to produce more
When you update content based on user feedback

questions.explanation: Content creation becomes harmful when: creating content compulsively to avoid other responsibilities, self-worth entirely tied to performance metrics (views, likes), neglecting relationships or self-care to produce more, using content to avoid processing emotions, or experiencing burnout from unsustainable schedules. These patterns suggest unhealthy relationship with content creation. Quality over quantity and updating based on feedback are healthy practices; compulsion and validation-seeking are warning signs.

title: Creating Lasting Educational Resources and Content - Quiz
